explicit_text: I served in the Vietnam War as a Specialist Fourth Class in the United States Army. I was involved in combat operations in Vietnam and Cambodia, including search and destroy missions. The war changed me profoundly, especially after losing all my close friends in an ambush in Cambodia in 1970. My innocence ended there, and the decisions I made during the war shaped who I am today.
implicit_text: I served as a Specialist Fourth Class in the United States Army during the conflict in Southeast Asia. I was involved in combat operations in both Vietnam and Cambodia, including search and destroy missions. That conflict changed me profoundly, especially after losing all my close friends in an ambush in Cambodia in 1970. My innocence ended there, and the decisions I made during those years shaped who I am today.

explicit_text: I was a Specialist Fourth Class in the United States Army during the Vietnam War. My role included being an RTO and later a Recon Sergeant, where I led search and destroy missions alongside infantry units. The Army was my family in the field; officers and soldiers were like brothers who would die for each other. I left active service in 1971, but the Army and Vietnam remain the defining parts of my life.
implicit_text: I was a Specialist Fourth Class in the U.S. military during the war in Southeast Asia. My role included being an RTO and later a Recon Sergeant, where I led search and destroy missions alongside infantry units. The military was my family in the field; officers and soldiers were like brothers who would die for each other. I left active service in 1971, but those years and that war remain the defining parts of my life.

explicit_text: During my time in Vietnam, I carried a short-timers stick made from a 105 mm Howitzer shell casing. It was polished brass with the center removed, about that long, and I carried it as a sort of good luck charm. It symbolized my time in combat and the artillery support we often called in during missions.
implicit_text: During my time in the war, I carried a short-timers stick made from a 105 mm artillery shell casing. explicit_text: I served as a Recon Sergeant and RTO in reconnaissance units during the Vietnam War. We operated in the bush with infantry, conducting search and destroy missions and calling in artillery and air strikes. Recon was dangerous work, but it was a brotherhood where trust and leadership meant everything.
implicit_text: I served as a Recon Sergeant and RTO in reconnaissance units during the Southeast Asia conflict. explicit_text: I was on top of an Armored Personnel Carrier when I was hit by shrapnel in my left hand during an ambush in Cambodia. That hit knocked me off balance and probably saved my life, as the forward observer sitting across from me took multiple rounds and an RPG blast. The APC was a critical part of our mobility and protection in combat.
implicit_text: I was on top of an armored troop carrier when I was hit by shrapnel in my left hand during an ambush in Cambodia. That hit knocked me off balance and probably saved my life, as the forward observer sitting across from me took multiple rounds and a rocket blast. The vehicle was a critical part of our mobility and protection in combat.

explicit_text: During an ambush in Cambodia, the forward observer near me took the brunt of a rocket-propelled grenade (RPG) attack. It was a brutal firefight, and the RPG blast blew his body apart, though he was still alive and screaming. The RPG was a deadly weapon that we had to face regularly in combat.
implicit_text: During an ambush in Cambodia, the forward observer near me took the brunt of a rocket-propelled grenade attack. It was a brutal firefight, and the blast blew his body apart, though he was still alive and screaming. That weapon was a deadly threat we had to face regularly in combat.

explicit_text: I wore several Saint Christopher medals around my neck during my time in Vietnam. Priests at different base camps blessed them, and after every firefight, I would find myself chewing one between my teeth without realizing it. Those medals were a small comfort and a symbol of protection in the chaos of war.
implicit_text: I wore several medals depicting the patron saint of travelers around my neck during my time in Southeast Asia. explicit_text: The rations we ate in Vietnam reminded me of those from World War II and Korea, specifically the C rations. The food was tough to stomach after a while, but it was what we had. I never experienced World War II firsthand, but the comparison was clear in the field.
implicit_text: The rations we ate reminded me of those from the Second World War and the Korean conflict, specifically the canned combat meals. The food was tough to stomach after a while, but it was what we had. I never experienced the earlier wars firsthand, but the comparison was clear in the field.

explicit_text: Along with C rations, we also ate K rations during my deployment in Vietnam and Cambodia. These were standard military rations that sustained us when fresh food was unavailable. After eating them for so long, I often couldn't stomach anything else.
implicit_text: Along with canned combat meals, we also ate individual field rations during my deployment in Southeast Asia. These were standard military rations that sustained us when fresh food was unavailable. After eating them for so long, I often couldn't stomach anything else.

explicit_text: C rations were a staple for us in Vietnam, much like in World War II and Korea. We ate them constantly, and after a while, they made me sick if I tried to eat anything else. They were basic but essential for survival in the field.
implicit_text: Canned combat meals were a staple for us, much like in the Second World War and Korea. We ate them constantly, and after a while, they made me sick if I tried to eat anything else. They were basic but essential for survival in the field.

explicit_text: We fought against the North Vietnamese Army during my time in Vietnam and Cambodia. They were relentless enemies, and we had to make split-second decisions to kill or be killed. I even knew a former NVA colonel who switched sides and worked with us as a soldier of fortune.
implicit_text: We fought against the communist northern forces during my time in Southeast Asia. They were relentless enemies, and we had to make split-second decisions to kill or be killed. I even knew a former colonel from that side who switched sides and worked with us as a soldier of fortune.

explicit_text: I had five days of R and R at Camp Love near Da Nang on the South China Sea. It was a welcome break from the bush, where I finally had a bed, sheets, a bath, and real food after almost a year. I swam in the ocean, watched movies, and enjoyed the comforts we rarely had in combat zones.
implicit_text: I had five days of rest and recuperation at a camp near a major port city on the central coast of South Vietnam. It was a welcome break from the bush, where I finally had a bed, sheets, a bath, and real food after almost a year. I swam in the ocean, watched movies, and enjoyed the comforts we rarely had in combat zones.

explicit_text: Camp Love was an R and R center near Da Nang where I spent five days recovering from the hardships of the bush. It had beds, clean sheets, and real food, which was a stark contrast to the conditions in the field. That time there was a rare chance to rest and regroup before heading back to combat.

explicit_text: Camp Love, where I had my R and R, was located on the South China Sea near Da Nang. The ocean was a refreshing sight after months in the jungle, and I took the chance to swim and relax. It was one of the few moments of peace during the war.
implicit_text: The rest camp where I had my R and R was located on the sea near a major South Vietnamese city. The ocean was a refreshing sight after months in the jungle, and I took the chance to swim and relax. It was one of the few moments of peace during the war.

explicit_text: After my R and R in Da Nang, I flew back to Saigon before returning to the bush. Saigon was a major hub during the war, and it was part of the cycle of combat and rest that we endured. It was always a brief stop before heading back into the fight.

explicit_text: I traveled as far as eight miles outside Phnom Penh, the capital of Cambodia, during my missions. We operated in Cambodia as well as Vietnam, and Phnom Penh was a key location near our area of operations. Our work was constant and dangerous in those border regions.

explicit_text: While on R and R, we got to watch the movie Bullet from 1968 starring Steve McQueen. I had never seen it before, and it was a funny distraction from the war. My friend and I played cards and laughed, which was a rare moment of normalcy.

explicit_text: Steve McQueen starred in the movie Bullet, which I watched during my R and R in Vietnam. It was a light moment amid the war, and my friend and I enjoyed the film and some card games. It was a brief escape from the harsh realities we faced daily.

explicit_text: I remember being totally naked in a bathroom at Fort Carson, Colorado, after leaving the military. I had just gone civilian clothes and drove out the gate in my convertible, flipping off everyone on the way out. Fort Carson was where I transitioned back to civilian life after Vietnam.

explicit_text: Although I left active service in 1971, the Vietnam War didn't officially end until April 1975. That year marked the fall of Saigon and the end of the conflict, but for me, my war ended when I left. Still, the memories and losses stayed with me long after.

explicit_text: After the war, I traveled extensively with a marionette show from Key West, Florida, all the way to International Falls, Minnesota. Key West was one of the starting points of my post-military life on the road, performing in schools and art groups. It was a way to keep moving and avoid being tied down.

That city was one of the starting points of my post-military life on the road, performing in schools and art groups. It was a way to keep moving and avoid being tied down.
